companyCaseStudy.title: How Cloudflare Absorbed a 1.3 Tbps DDoS: Anycast, Edge Scrubbing, and Keeping Customers Online
companyCaseStudy.summary: When attackers launched a 1.3 Tbps volumetric DDoS, Cloudflare’s edge-first architecture — 200+ POPs, Anycast routing, and multi-stage scrubbing — kept customer origins online with millisecond-level latency impact. This case study walks through the problem, the exact engineering choices, and the numbers behind the mitigation.
companyCaseStudy.explanation:
## Hook

A single attack peaked at ~1.3 terabits-per-second — enough to drown many networks — but Cloudflare’s globally-distributed edge absorbed and scrubbed the traffic, protecting customers without sending the flood upstream.

## Problem: the flood and the stakes

On [the incident], a traffic flood reached ~1.3 Tbps and included both massive volumetric UDP/ICMP/CLDAP-style amplification and an HTTP layer probing for resources. For many online services, an attack of this scale means either origin networks saturate (bringing everyone down) or expensive emergency upstream filtering — both unacceptable for paying customers during peak business hours.

Numbers at a glance:

- Attack peak: ~1.3 Tbps of attack traffic
- Global POPs in play: 200+ (anycast edge)
- Legitimate peak RPS handled in the same window: millions of HTTP requests/sec
- Time-to-effective-mitigation: typically measured in seconds-to-minutes (automated edge rules)

## Challenge: scale, speed, and collateral damage

The engineering challenges were:

- Preventing upstream saturation of customer ISPs and cloud links
- Detecting and distinguishing legitimate traffic patterns from attack traffic in real-time
- Applying mitigations without creating high false-positive rates that block real users
- Doing all of the above at terabit scale with low added latency

## Solution: edge-first Anycast + layered scrubbing

Cloudflare’s approach is a multi-layered defense built into the network fabric:

1. Anycast global edge (200+ PoPs): advertise the same prefixes from many locations so attack traffic is spread across the wide-area fabric instead of concentrating on one pipe.
2. Fast packet handling in-kernel/XDP/eBPF at the edge: drop or rate-limit at line rate before packets consume expensive user-space cycles.
3. Layered scrubbing pipeline per PoP:
   - L3/4 filters (access control, SYN cookies, UDP rate limits)
   - L7 HTTP inspection & rate-limiting (challenge pages, bot management)
   - Cache before origin: obscure or static resources served from cache reduce origin load
4. Automated rule generation + telemetry: when anomalies are detected, automatic mitigations (rate-limits, challenge pages, custom rules) are applied and adjusted based on live telemetry.
5. Central orchestration for global signals: correlate indicators across POPs to avoid localized oscillations and reduce false positives.

### Architecture (simplified)

```mermaid
graph LR
  Client[End Users (legit + attack)] -->|BGP Anycast| Internet[Internet]
  Internet --> PoP[Cloudflare PoP (200+ POPs)]
  PoP --> EdgeFilter[Edge Filters (XDP/eBPF, ACLs, SYN cookies)]
  EdgeFilter --> Cache[HTTP Cache / CDN]
  EdgeFilter --> Scrub[Traffic Scrubber / L7 Inspection]
  Scrub --> Origin[Customer Origin (if needed)]
  Cache --> Client[(Served from Cache)]
  Scrub -->|Cleaned Traffic| Origin
```

## Why these choices worked

- Anycast turns a concentrated attack into many smaller ones. Instead of one 1.3 Tbps pipe, the attack is distributed across hundreds of POPs, turning a single catastrophic event into hundreds of manageable floods.
- Doing most filtering at kernel/XDP/eBPF levels means decisions happen at 10s–100s of Gbps per server with minimal CPU overhead.
- Caching and challenge-response protects origin capacity: many requests never reach the origin.
- Automated telemetry and global correlation means mitigations are quick and precise; manual ops don’t have to write rules for each POP.

## Before / After (impact metrics)

| Metric | Before (attack starts) | After (edge mitigation) |
|--------|------------------------:|------------------------:|
| Peak attack throughput observed | 1.3 Tbps | Attack absorbed at edge (0 Tbps to origin)
| Time to effective mitigation | Minutes without automation | Seconds–minutes with automated rules
| Customer origin saturation | High risk (would saturate links) | Near-zero (most traffic dropped/scrubbed at PoPs)
| End-user latency impact | Potentially huge | Small increase (tens of ms) for challenged flows
| Global availability for protected customers | Risk of downtime | 99.99%+ during event (no large outages reported)

## Results & operational lessons

- The distributed-anycast + edge-scrub model prevents single-link saturation and keeps most customers online.
- Fast in-kernel packet handling (XDP/eBPF) reduces mitigation cost and avoids CPU exhaustion on edge machines.
- Cache-first policies drastically cut origin load during L7 floods: many requests are served without touching the origin.
- Automation + global correlation reduces mean time to mitigation from human minutes to automated seconds, while lowering false positives.

Cloudflare’s public write-ups note these events as tests of the network fabric — the real win was keeping customers online without manual interventions and without requiring customers to incur upstream filtering costs.

## Tactics you can apply (for your infra)

- If you control ingress prefixes: use Anycast (or replicate ingress) so traffic is distributed.
- Push filters as close to the edge as possible (ideally before your expensive or limited connectors).
- Automate mitigation rules based on short-window telemetry; manual-only processes are too slow.
- Cache aggressively: CDN/cache-before-origin is your best friend during HTTP floods.

## Caveats

- Anycast relies on comprehensive global presence — a small provider can’t trivially replicate this model.
- Automated rules must be tuned to avoid blocking valid traffic; invest in telemetry and rollback logic.

## Final note

This is an example of an engineering problem where architecture (global distribution + local, fast filtering) is as important as clever per-request logic. At terabit scale, the right placement of controls — at the edge — is what keeps services live.
keyTakeaways: Distribute the problem: Anycast + 200+ POPs turns one massive pipe into many manageable ones (1.3 Tbps → spread load).
Filter early and cheaply: use kernel-level packet handling (XDP/eBPF) and per-POP scrubbing to avoid CPU/IO exhaustion and upstream saturation.
Cache and automate: serve what you can from cache and use automated, telemetry-driven mitigations to reduce time-to-protection to seconds.

concept.title: The API Gateway Pattern: Your Services' Receptionist, Doorman, and Traffic Cop
concept.summary: An API Gateway provides a single entrypoint for clients into a system of microservices, consolidating cross-cutting concerns like auth, routing, aggregation, rate-limiting and protocol translation. It simplifies client interactions and reduces duplicated logic in services—at the cost of an extra network hop and a potential central chokepoint if misused.
concept.explanation:
## Why it matters

When a company breaks a monolith into microservices, clients suddenly must talk to many different endpoints with different auth, versions, and payload shapes. That quickly becomes a mess: mobile apps must implement multiple flows, security and observability logic is duplicated, and every client change can ripple across many teams.

An *API Gateway* acts like a smart receptionist in a large office building: the visitor (client) goes to one desk, the receptionist checks ID, routes them to the right floor, sometimes fills out a short form (aggregation), and enforces building rules (rate-limits, auth). The receptionist makes the visitors' lives easier and keeps the building secure and predictable.

### High-level responsibilities

- Centralized authentication and authorization
- Request routing to the appropriate backend service (or version)
- Protocol translation (e.g., HTTP -> gRPC) or payload transformation
- Response aggregation / composition (combine multiple service results)
- Cross-cutting policies: rate limiting, caching, monitoring, tracing headers
- Edge concerns: TLS termination, CORS


## How it works (visual)

### Request flow (flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  C[Client] --> G[API Gateway]
  G -->|Auth & Rate Limit| A{Auth OK?}
  A -->|Yes| R[Route & Transform]
  A -->|No| E[401/429]
  R --> S1[Service A]
  R --> S2[Service B]
  S1 --> G
  S2 --> G
  G -->|Aggregate| C
```

### Typical sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  Client->>Gateway: Request /orders/123
  Gateway->>AuthSvc: Validate token
  AuthSvc-->>Gateway: OK
  Gateway->>OrderSvc: Get order 123
  Gateway->>InventorySvc: Get item statuses
  InventorySvc-->>Gateway: Items ready
  OrderSvc-->>Gateway: Order data
  Gateway-->>Client: Combined response
```


## Before / After: Why a Gateway helps

| Scenario | Pros | Cons | When to Use |
|----------|------|------|-------------|
| Clients talk to each service directly | No single point of failure; direct path | Clients must know many endpoints, auth repeated, duplicated logic | Small systems or internal-only APIs |
| Add API Gateway (single entry) | Centralized auth/rate limit, easier client integration, response composition | Extra hop, potential bottleneck if overused | Public APIs, many clients, cross-cutting concerns |

### Concrete BEFORE / AFTER example

| Aspect | BEFORE (many endpoints) | AFTER (API Gateway) |
|--------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Client complexity | Implement auth and call many URLs | Call one URL, get combined data |
| Security | Each service must implement auth | Centralized auth in gateway |
| Versioning | Clients need to know service versions | Gateway routes to appropriate service version |


## Code examples (practical)

### Simple Node.js Express gateway that routes, checks a token, and aggregates

```js
const express = require('express')
const fetch = require('node-fetch')
const app = express()

// very small auth middleware
function auth(req, res, next) {
  const token = req.headers['authorization']
  if (!token || token !== 'Bearer super-secret') return res.status(401).json({error:'unauthorized'})
  next()
}

app.use(auth)

// simple proxy route
app.get('/orders/:id', async (req, res) => {
  const id = req.params.id
  const order = await fetch(`http://order-svc/internal/orders/${id}`).then(r=>r.json())
  const inventory = await fetch(`http://inventory-svc/internal/items?ids=${order.items.join(',')}`).then(r=>r.json())
  // compose response
  order.items = order.items.map(i => ({ id:i, status: inventory[i] }))
  res.json(order)
})

app.listen(8080)
```

### NGINX as a lightweight gateway config (TLS termination + proxy)

```nginx
server {
  listen 443 ssl;
  server_name api.example.com;

  ssl_certificate /etc/ssl/fullchain.pem;
  ssl_certificate_key /etc/ssl/privkey.pem;

  location /orders/ {
    proxy_pass http://order-gateway:8080/;
    proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $remote_addr;
  }

  location /inventory/ {
    proxy_pass http://inventory-gateway:8080/;
  }
}
```


## When to use an API Gateway

- You have many clients (web, mobile, third-party) that need a consistent, stable API surface.
- You want centralized security, rate limiting, monitoring, or protocol translation.
- You need to compose data from multiple services to keep client round-trips low.

When you *don't* need it:
- Small team/simple internal systems where adding another component increases complexity.
- If you're replacing one monolith with another centralized bottleneck without careful design.


## Common pitfalls and how to avoid them

- **Gateway becomes a monolith / does too much**: keep the gateway focused on edge concerns. Offload heavy business logic or orchestration to dedicated aggregation services (API composition) or backend-for-frontend (BFF) per client type.
- **Single point of failure**: run gateways statelessly and scale horizontally behind a load balancer; use health checks and auto-scaling.
- **Increased latency**: each call goes through the gateway. Minimize extra hops by using keep-alive connections and co-locating gateways near backends; use async aggregation or caching for frequent reads.
- **Versioning and coupling**: avoid baking service-specific paths into clients. Use the gateway to route by version or host route-specific BFFs.
- **Too many responsibilities** (auth, caching, composition, logging, transformations, business rules): prioritize and split responsibilities. Consider a service mesh for service-to-service concerns and let the gateway handle north-south traffic.
- **Testing and observability**: instrument the gateway—trace IDs, structured logs, and metrics—so you can see where time is spent and which rules fire.


## Patterns and alternatives

- *Backend-for-Frontend (BFF)*: a specialized gateway per client type (mobile, web) that handles client-specific shaping.
- *Service Mesh*: handles service-to-service (east-west) concerns like mTLS, retries, and telemetry; complements the gateway.
- *Edge proxies / CDN*: offload caching and static responses at the CDN edge to reduce load on the gateway.


## Quick checklist before you add a gateway

- Do clients currently suffer from repeated auth/logic or many endpoints?
- Can you keep the gateway thin and stateless?
- Do you have autoscaling and observability in place?
- Are you prepared to split responsibilities later if the gateway grows too big?


## Final thought

An API Gateway is a powerful pattern for simplifying client interactions and centralizing cross-cutting concerns, but like a good receptionist, it must know its role: route, vet, and direct — not run the whole building. Design it intentionally, monitor it closely, and keep it focused.
keyTakeaways: Use an API Gateway to centralize cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate-limiting, routing, aggregation) and simplify clients.
Keep gateways thin and stateless; offload heavy orchestration to dedicated composition services or BFFs.
Avoid making the gateway a single-point-of-failure or a catch-all for business logic—scale, instrument, and split responsibilities when it grows.
